DL3002.title: Last USER should not be root
DL3002.why: Running a container as root means if an attacker breaks out of the app, they have full control over the container and possibly the host. The principle of least privilege says: only give the minimum access needed.
DL3002.fix:
Add these two lines near the END of your Dockerfile:
  RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
  USER appuser
DL3002.learnMore: Study: Linux user permissions, principle of least privilege, Docker security best practices

DL3008.title: Pin versions in apt-get install
DL3008.why: Without pinned versions, every time the image is built it may install a different version of a package. This makes builds non-reproducible and can introduce unexpected bugs or security vulnerabilities.
DL3008.fix: Pin the version: instead of  RUN apt-get install -y curl  use  RUN apt-get install -y curl=7.68.0-1ubuntu2
DL3008.learnMore: Study: Reproducible builds, semantic versioning, apt package pinning

DL3009.title: Delete apt-get lists after install
DL3009.why: apt-get stores package index files in /var/lib/apt/lists/. These files are only needed during installation. Leaving them in the image wastes space (sometimes 50-100 MB).
DL3009.fix:
Always end your apt-get block with:
  && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
Full example:
  RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
DL3009.learnMore: Study: Docker image layer optimisation, multi-stage builds

DL3015.title: Avoid installing unnecessary packages
DL3015.why: Packages you don't need increase the image size, add more potential CVEs to scan, and give attackers more tools if they break in.
DL3015.fix:
Use the --no-install-recommends flag:
  RUN apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl
DL3015.learnMore: Study: Docker image minimisation, distroless images, Alpine Linux

DL3025.title: Use exec form for CMD and ENTRYPOINT
DL3025.why: Shell form (CMD python app.py) spawns a shell process as PID 1. Exec form (CMD ["python", "app.py"]) runs your process as PID 1 directly. PID 1 receives OS signals like SIGTERM, so exec form lets Docker gracefully stop your app.
DL3025.fix:
Change shell form to exec form:
  # Bad:  CMD python app.py
  # Good: CMD ["python", "app.py"]
DL3025.learnMore: Study: Linux signals, PID 1, graceful container shutdown

DL3006.title: Always tag the FROM image
DL3006.why: FROM ubuntu with no tag silently uses :latest. This means tomorrow's build might pull a different OS version and break your app. Always pin to a specific version.
DL3006.fix:
Add a specific version tag:
  # Bad:  FROM ubuntu
  # Good: FROM ubuntu:22.04
DL3006.learnMore: Study: Reproducible builds, semantic versioning, Docker image tags

DL3007.title: Avoid using :latest tag
DL3007.why: The :latest tag is a floating pointer. It changes whenever the image maintainer pushes a new version. Your build will silently change behaviour, break, or introduce vulnerabilities.
DL3007.fix:
Pin to a specific version:
  # Bad:  FROM node:latest
  # Good: FROM node:20-alpine
DL3007.learnMore: Study: Image tag pinning, supply chain security, reproducible builds

DL3014.title: Use -y flag with apt-get
DL3014.why: Without -y, apt-get waits for user input to confirm installation. In a Docker build there is no user, so the build hangs forever and eventually times out.
DL3014.fix:
Always add -y:
  RUN apt-get install -y curl
DL3014.learnMore: Study: Non-interactive shell scripts, Docker build process

DL4006.title: Set SHELL option -o pipefail
DL4006.why: When you use pipes (|) in RUN commands, bash only returns the exit code of the LAST command. If an earlier command fails, the error is silently ignored and the build succeeds when it shouldn't.
DL4006.fix:
Add this before any RUN command that uses pipes:
  SHELL ["/bin/bash", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]
  RUN curl https://example.com | tar xz
DL4006.learnMore: Study: Bash pipefail, shell error handling, set -e

DL3001.title: Avoid platform-specific commands
DL3001.why: Commands like apt-get are specific to Debian/Ubuntu. If someone builds your Dockerfile on a different base image or OS, the command will fail. Try to write portable commands or document the base OS requirement.
DL3001.fix:
Be explicit about the base image OS and document it:
  FROM ubuntu:22.04
  # Requires Debian/Ubuntu — uses apt-get
DL3001.learnMore: Study: Docker base images, distroless containers, Alpine Linux

DL3010.title: Use ADD for archives, not COPY
DL3010.why: ADD has special behaviour: it automatically extracts .tar.gz archives. COPY is a simple file copy with no magic. Using ADD for plain files is confusing because readers expect extraction behaviour.
DL3010.fix:
Use COPY for plain files, ADD only for archives you want auto-extracted:
  COPY app.js /app/
  ADD app.tar.gz /app/
DL3010.learnMore: Study: Docker COPY vs ADD, Dockerfile best practices

DL3020.title: Use COPY instead of ADD for plain files
DL3020.why: ADD with a local file path does the same thing as COPY but hides the intent. ADD can also fetch from URLs, which is a security risk (no checksum validation). COPY is explicit and safe.
DL3020.fix:
Replace ADD with COPY for local files:
  # Bad:  ADD ./src /app/src
  # Good: COPY ./src /app/src
DL3020.learnMore: Study: Docker Dockerfile best practices, supply chain security

AVD-AZU-0002.title: Azure Storage Account not enforcing HTTPS
AVD-AZU-0002.why: If HTTPS is not enforced, data can be transmitted in plaintext over HTTP. An attacker on the same network can read your data (man-in-the-middle attack).
AVD-AZU-0002.learnMore: Study: TLS/HTTPS, man-in-the-middle attacks, Azure storage security

AVD-AZU-0007.title: Azure VM has open management ports
AVD-AZU-0007.why: Ports like SSH (22) and RDP (3389) open to the internet (0.0.0.0/0) are constantly scanned by bots. They will attempt brute-force attacks within minutes of opening the port.
AVD-AZU-0007.fix:
Restrict source to your specific IP or VPN CIDR:
  source_address_prefix = "10.0.0.0/8"  # internal network only
  # NOT: source_address_prefix = "0.0.0.0/0"
AVD-AZU-0007.learnMore: Study: Network security groups, least privilege networking, VPN/bastion hosts

AVD-AZU-0016.title: Azure NSG allows unrestricted inbound traffic
AVD-AZU-0016.why: A rule with source 0.0.0.0/0 (any IP) on any port allows the entire internet to reach your resource. This is almost never intentional and exposes your service to attacks.
AVD-AZU-0016.fix:
Change source_address_prefix from 0.0.0.0/0 to a specific CIDR:
  source_address_prefix = "10.0.0.0/16"
AVD-AZU-0016.learnMore: Study: Azure NSG rules, CIDR notation, zero-trust networking

AVD-AZU-0017.title: Azure NSG allows unrestricted egress
AVD-AZU-0017.why: Unrestricted outbound means a compromised VM can call home to attacker-controlled servers, exfiltrate data, or be used in DDoS attacks. Limiting egress is defence-in-depth.
AVD-AZU-0017.fix:
Restrict egress to known destinations:
  direction = "Outbound"
  destination_address_prefix = "10.0.0.0/8"  # internal only
AVD-AZU-0017.learnMore: Study: Egress filtering, defence in depth, data exfiltration prevention

AVD-AWS-0001.title: AWS S3 bucket is publicly accessible
AVD-AWS-0001.why: A public S3 bucket means anyone on the internet can read (or write) your files. Thousands of data breaches have occurred because of misconfigured public S3 buckets.
AVD-AWS-0001.learnMore: Study: S3 bucket policies, AWS public access block, data breach case studies

AVD-AWS-0086.title: AWS Security Group allows unrestricted ingress
AVD-AWS-0086.why: A security group with 0.0.0.0/0 as source exposes your EC2 instances to the entire internet. Even if your app is not ready, attackers will probe any open port.
AVD-AWS-0086.fix:
Use specific CIDR blocks:
  cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]  # VPC internal only
  # Or specify your office IP: ["203.0.113.50/32"]
AVD-AWS-0086.learnMore: Study: AWS VPC security groups, least privilege networking, CIDR notation

AVD-AWS-0107.title: AWS RDS instance not encrypted
AVD-AWS-0107.why: Unencrypted RDS means if someone gets physical or logical access to the storage, they can read your database files in plaintext. Encryption at rest protects against storage-level attacks.
AVD-AWS-0107.learnMore: Study: Encryption at rest, AWS KMS, database security

AVD-GCP-0012.title: GCP compute instance has a public IP
AVD-GCP-0012.why: A public IP makes the instance directly reachable from the internet. Without a load balancer or firewall in front, every port is potentially exposed.
AVD-GCP-0012.fix:
Remove the public IP and use a load balancer or Cloud NAT for outbound:
  # Remove access_config {} block from network_interface to disable public IP
AVD-GCP-0012.learnMore: Study: GCP VPC, Cloud NAT, private Google Access, zero-trust networking

CKV_DOCKER_2.title: Ensure HEALTHCHECK is set in Dockerfile
CKV_DOCKER_2.why: Without a HEALTHCHECK, Docker and Kubernetes cannot tell if your app is actually working. The container might be 'running' but serving errors. Health checks enable automatic restarts.
CKV_DOCKER_2.fix:
Add a HEALTHCHECK instruction:
  HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s \
    CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
CKV_DOCKER_2.learnMore: Study: Docker HEALTHCHECK, Kubernetes liveness probes, container health monitoring

CKV_DOCKER_3.title: Ensure container does not run as root user
CKV_DOCKER_3.why: See DL3002 — running as root is dangerous because it gives an attacker maximum privileges inside the container.
CKV_DOCKER_3.fix:
Create and switch to a non-root user:
  RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
  USER app
CKV_DOCKER_3.learnMore: Study: Linux user namespaces, container security, principle of least privilege

CKV_DOCKER_4.title: Do not use ADD with remote URLs
CKV_DOCKER_4.why: ADD fetches remote URLs without any signature or checksum verification. An attacker who intercepts the download (or compromises the remote server) can replace the file with malware.
CKV_DOCKER_4.fix:
Use curl/wget with a checksum instead:
  RUN curl -fsSL https://example.com/file.tar.gz -o /tmp/file.tar.gz \
    && echo "expected-sha256  /tmp/file.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c
CKV_DOCKER_4.learnMore: Study: Supply chain attacks, checksum verification, secure downloads

CKV_K8S_8.title: Liveness probe should be configured
CKV_K8S_8.why: Without a liveness probe, Kubernetes cannot detect if your app is stuck in a deadlock or infinite loop. The pod keeps running but serving nothing — users get errors until someone notices.
CKV_K8S_8.fix:
Add a livenessProbe to your container spec:
  livenessProbe:
    httpGet:
      path: /health
      port: 3000
    initialDelaySeconds: 15
    periodSeconds: 20
CKV_K8S_8.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes probes, self-healing systems, container health monitoring

CKV_K8S_9.title: Readiness probe should be configured
CKV_K8S_9.why: Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes sends traffic to a pod before it is ready (e.g., still loading config or connecting to a database). Users see errors during startup or rolling updates.
CKV_K8S_9.fix:
Add a readinessProbe:
  readinessProbe:
    httpGet:
      path: /ready
      port: 3000
    initialDelaySeconds: 5
    periodSeconds: 10
CKV_K8S_9.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes readiness vs liveness probes, rolling deployments, zero-downtime updates

CKV_K8S_10.title: CPU limits should be set
CKV_K8S_10.why: Without CPU limits, one misbehaving pod can consume all CPU on a node and starve other pods. This causes cascading failures across the cluster.
CKV_K8S_10.fix:
Set CPU limits in your container resources:
  resources:
    limits:
      cpu: "500m"     # 0.5 CPU cores
CKV_K8S_10.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes resource management, CPU throttling, QoS classes

CKV_K8S_11.title: CPU requests should be set
CKV_K8S_11.why: CPU requests tell the Kubernetes scheduler how much CPU to reserve for the pod. Without them, the scheduler places pods randomly and nodes can become overloaded.
CKV_K8S_11.fix:
Set CPU requests:
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: "250m"     # 0.25 CPU cores
CKV_K8S_11.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes scheduling, resource requests vs limits, cluster capacity planning

CKV_K8S_12.title: Memory limits should be set
CKV_K8S_12.why: Without memory limits, a memory leak in one pod can exhaust all memory on a node, causing the OS to kill random processes (OOM killer). Other pods lose data.
CKV_K8S_12.fix:
Set memory limits:
  resources:
    limits:
      memory: "128Mi"
CKV_K8S_12.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes OOM killer, memory management, container resource limits

CKV_K8S_13.title: Memory requests should be set
CKV_K8S_13.why: Memory requests tell the scheduler the minimum memory a pod needs. Without them, the scheduler may place a pod on a node that cannot support it.
CKV_K8S_13.fix:
Set memory requests:
  resources:
    requests:
      memory: "64Mi"
CKV_K8S_13.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes resource requests, pod scheduling, node capacity

CKV_K8S_14.title: Image should not use the :latest tag
CKV_K8S_14.why: The :latest tag changes silently. A rolling update that pulls a new :latest may introduce a breaking change or vulnerability. Pin to a specific immutable tag.
CKV_K8S_14.fix:
Use a specific version tag:
  # Bad:  image: myapp:latest
  # Good: image: myapp:1.2.3
CKV_K8S_14.learnMore: Study: Image tag pinning, GitOps, immutable infrastructure

CKV_K8S_15.title: Image pull policy should be Always
CKV_K8S_15.why: IfNotPresent means Kubernetes will use a cached old image if one exists. If you push a security fix with the same tag, nodes that already have the old image won't pull the fix.
CKV_K8S_15.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes image pull policies, rolling deployments, image caching

CKV_K8S_16.title: Container should not run as root
CKV_K8S_16.why: A root container can read sensitive host files, modify the filesystem, and escalate privileges outside the container if the kernel has a vulnerability.
CKV_K8S_16.fix:
Set runAsNonRoot in the security context:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
CKV_K8S_16.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes security context, container isolation, privilege escalation

CKV_K8S_28.title: Minimise containers with added capabilities
CKV_K8S_28.why: Linux capabilities break down root privileges into smaller units. Adding extra capabilities (like NET_ADMIN or SYS_PTRACE) gives an attacker more attack surface if they break into the container.
CKV_K8S_28.fix:
Drop all capabilities and only add back what you need:
  securityContext:
    capabilities:
      drop: ["ALL"]
      add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]  # only if needed
CKV_K8S_28.learnMore: Study: Linux capabilities, principle of least privilege, container security

CKV_K8S_30.title: Apply security context to your pod
CKV_K8S_30.why: Without a security context, containers run with default (often too permissive) settings. A security context explicitly defines what the container can and cannot do.
CKV_K8S_30.fix:
Add securityContext at both pod and container level:
  spec:
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      runAsUser: 1000
    containers:
    - securityContext:
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
CKV_K8S_30.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes Pod Security Standards, security context, seccomp profiles

CKV_TF_1.title: Terraform module source should use a commit hash
CKV_TF_1.why: Module sources using branch names (ref=main) are mutable — the module code can change without you knowing. A commit hash is immutable and guarantees you always get the same code.
CKV_TF_1.fix:
Pin module to a commit hash:
  source = "git::https://github.com/org/module.git?ref=a1b2c3d"
CKV_TF_1.learnMore: Study: Supply chain security, Terraform module pinning, GitOps

CKV_AZURE_1.title: Azure Storage Account should enforce HTTPS
CKV_AZURE_1.why: HTTP transmits data in plaintext. Anyone who can intercept network traffic between your app and storage can read or modify the data.
CKV_AZURE_1.learnMore: Study: Transport Layer Security, man-in-the-middle attacks, Azure storage encryption

CKV_AZURE_2.title: Azure managed disk should be encrypted
CKV_AZURE_2.why: Unencrypted managed disks can be read directly if an attacker gains access to the underlying storage. Encryption at rest protects data from physical and logical storage attacks.
CKV_AZURE_2.learnMore: Study: Azure disk encryption, encryption at rest, key management

runAsNonRoot.title: Pod must not run as root
runAsNonRoot.why: If a container runs as root (UID 0) and an attacker exploits a vulnerability in your application, they get root access inside the container — potentially allowing host escape.
runAsNonRoot.fix:
Add to spec.securityContext or container.securityContext:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
runAsNonRoot.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes Pod Security Standards, user namespaces, privilege escalation

readOnlyRootFilesystem.title: Container filesystem should be read-only
readOnlyRootFilesystem.why: A writable root filesystem lets malware write scripts or modify binaries inside the container. Read-only root filesystem is a key defence against many attack patterns.
readOnlyRootFilesystem.fix:
Set readOnlyRootFilesystem in the container security context:
  securityContext:
    readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  # Mount a writable volume for runtime data:
  volumeMounts:
  - name: tmp
    mountPath: /tmp
readOnlyRootFilesystem.learnMore: Study: Immutable infrastructure, container hardening, Kubernetes security context

cpuRequestsMissing.title: CPU requests not set
cpuRequestsMissing.why: The Kubernetes scheduler uses resource requests to decide which node to place a pod on. Without CPU requests, pods may be placed on overloaded nodes.
cpuRequestsMissing.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes resource requests, scheduler, QoS classes

cpuLimitsMissing.title: CPU limits not set
cpuLimitsMissing.why: Without CPU limits, a single runaway pod can consume all CPU on a node, affecting every other pod running there.
cpuLimitsMissing.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes CPU throttling, resource limits, noisy neighbour problem

memoryRequestsMissing.title: Memory requests not set
memoryRequestsMissing.why: Without memory requests, Kubernetes cannot guarantee the pod gets the memory it needs. On a constrained node, the pod may be OOM killed.
memoryRequestsMissing.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes memory management, OOM killer, QoS classes

memoryLimitsMissing.title: Memory limits not set
memoryLimitsMissing.why: A memory leak without limits will grow until the OS kills processes on the node, causing cascading failures. Limits cap the damage.
memoryLimitsMissing.learnMore: Study: Container memory limits, OOM killer, Kubernetes resource governance

livenessProbeMissing.title: Liveness probe not configured
livenessProbeMissing.why: Without a liveness probe, a deadlocked application keeps running forever. Kubernetes has no way to know it should restart the pod.
livenessProbeMissing.fix:
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 3000
  initialDelaySeconds: 15
  periodSeconds: 20
livenessProbeMissing.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes self-healing, liveness vs readiness probes, health check endpoints

readinessProbeMissing.title: Readiness probe not configured
readinessProbeMissing.why: Without a readiness probe, traffic is sent to a pod that hasn't finished starting up. Users see 503 errors during deployments.
readinessProbeMissing.fix:
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /ready
    port: 3000
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
readinessProbeMissing.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes rolling updates, zero-downtime deployments, readiness gates

pullPolicyNotAlways.title: Image pull policy not set to Always
pullPolicyNotAlways.why: IfNotPresent may reuse a cached old image after you push a security fix. Always ensures the latest image is pulled on every pod start.
pullPolicyNotAlways.learnMore: Study: Kubernetes image lifecycle, rolling updates, supply chain security

tagNotSpecified.title: Image using :latest or no tag specified
tagNotSpecified.why: Untagged or :latest images are not reproducible. You cannot roll back to a known-good state because :latest is a moving target.
tagNotSpecified.fix:
Pin to an immutable tag:
  image: myapp:1.2.3  # or a SHA digest: myapp@sha256:abc...
tagNotSpecified.learnMore: Study: Image immutability, GitOps, Kubernetes deployment strategies

insecureCapabilities.title: Dangerous Linux capabilities added to container
insecureCapabilities.why: Capabilities like SYS_ADMIN, NET_ADMIN, and SYS_PTRACE give broad kernel-level access. If an attacker exploits your app, they can use these capabilities to escalate to the host.
insecureCapabilities.fix:
Drop ALL capabilities and only add back what is strictly needed:
  securityContext:
    capabilities:
      drop: ["ALL"]
      add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]
insecureCapabilities.learnMore: Study: Linux capabilities man page, container privilege escalation, CAP_SYS_ADMIN risks

hostIPCSet.title: hostIPC should not be true
hostIPCSet.why: hostIPC: true gives the container access to the host's IPC namespace. It can read shared memory from other processes on the host, including other containers.
hostIPCSet.fix:
Remove or set to false:
  spec:
    hostIPC: false
hostIPCSet.learnMore: Study: Linux IPC namespaces, container isolation, Kubernetes security

hostPIDSet.title: hostPID should not be true
hostPIDSet.why: hostPID: true lets the container see and signal all processes on the host. An attacker could kill critical system processes or inspect sensitive process data.
hostPIDSet.fix:
Remove or set to false:
  spec:
    hostPID: false
hostPIDSet.learnMore: Study: Linux PID namespaces, container isolation, privilege escalation

hostNetworkSet.title: hostNetwork should not be true
hostNetworkSet.why: hostNetwork: true removes the network namespace isolation. The container shares the host's network stack and can listen on and connect to any host port.
hostNetworkSet.fix:
Remove or set to false:
  spec:
    hostNetwork: false
hostNetworkSet.learnMore: Study: Linux network namespaces, pod network isolation, Kubernetes CNI

privilegeEscalationAllowed.title: allowPrivilegeEscalation should be false
privilegeEscalationAllowed.why: If allowPrivilegeEscalation is true (the default), a process inside the container can use setuid binaries to gain more privileges than its parent — potentially becoming root.
privilegeEscalationAllowed.fix:
Explicitly disable it:
  securityContext:
    allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
privilegeEscalationAllowed.learnMore: Study: Linux setuid, privilege escalation, Kubernetes pod security standards
